title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Securities and Exchange Commission relating to "Form N-PORT and Form N-CEN Reporting; Guidance on Open-End Fund Liquidity Risk Management Programs".
plain_summary: Congress wants to cancel a rule made by the Securities and Exchange Commission. The rule tells investment funds how they must report information and handle the risk of running out of cash when people want their money back. If Congress passes this resolution, the SEC rule will be thrown out and will not take effect. This means investment funds would not have to follow the new reporting and cash management requirements.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Joint Resolution Congressional Disapproval
sections.text: Congress officially rejects a rule created by the Securities and Exchange Commission. The Securities and Exchange Commission is the government agency that watches over the stock market and investment companies. The rule they made is called Form N-PORT and Form N-CEN Reporting and includes guidance about how open-end funds should manage liquidity risk. Open-end funds are investment funds that people can buy into or sell out of at any time. Liquidity risk means the danger that the fund might not be able to quickly turn its investments into cash when people want their money back. This rule was published in the Federal Register on September eleventh, twenty twenty-four. Because Congress disapproves of this rule, it will have no power and cannot be enforced.
